observations: 2019 through mid-2020 shows a smooth annual cycle: rise to ~130 in spring 2019, dip to ~80 by autumn 2019, rise again to ~130 by early 2020, then decline toward ~85 by late summer 2020.
A sharp discontinuous jump occurs around 2020-09, where the level steps up from ~85 to ~120 abruptly and does not revert.
After the jump the series rises further via a second step/ramp around 2020-12 (to ~140) and continues climbing to ~190-200 by early 2021.
High-frequency oscillation (short-period wiggle) is present throughout, consistent with a regular short cycle riding on the slower movement.
The smooth large-amplitude annual swing that dominated 2019 appears much less pronounced after the upward shift; late history is mostly elevated level with small ripples.
Near the forecast origin (mid-2021) the level is high (~185-200) and shows a slight downward bend from its peak.
visual.pattern_summary: A previously seasonal series undergoes a permanent upward level shift (with a follow-on ramp) after which the strong annual cycle is no longer evident.
hypotheses: A forecast trained only on the elevated recent window may carry forward the high level but miss any remaining cyclical behavior or mean-revert incorrectly.
If the recent climb is read as a persistent trend, the forecast could extrapolate upward past the apparent late-history plateau/turn.
The pre-shift annual seasonality may no longer apply, so reintroducing the old cycle shape could mismatch the new regime.
The slight downturn right before the origin could be over- or under-weighted, biasing the near-term path.
uncertainties: Whether the late-2020 jump is a permanent regime change or a long-lived but temporary elevation.
Whether the recent upward movement is a continuing trend or has already peaked and is reverting.
The exact dates and magnitudes of the shift and subsequent ramp.
Whether a short cycle still exists after the shift or its amplitude has genuinely collapsed.
External causes of the discontinuity and whether they persist beyond the forecast origin.

proposal.visual_first_rationale: Visually, the chart shows two distinct phases of upward movement after mid-2019: first a gradual descent from ~130 to ~85 between mid-2019 and late-2019, then a steady climb from ~85 through ~120, ~140, and up to ~190-200 by early 2021. Critically, this post-trough ascent is not a clean vertical step — it is a progressive, multi-stage ramp spanning roughly 2019-07 through 2020-01 (and beyond), where the level rises incrementally rather than jumping instantaneously. The visual description explicitly notes "a second step/ramp around 2020-12" and a "continuing climb," reinforcing the gradual-drift interpretation rather than a single discrete jump. Reconciling with numerics: the `candidate_drift_intervals` diagnostic confirms exactly this — a drift interval from 2019-07-17 to 2020-01-02 with slope_per_step=0.132 and total_delta=22.3, representing a meaningful gradual transition between two levels. The `permanent_shift_magnitude` of 28.8 is large, but the visual and the drift interval together indicate this magnitude was accumulated via a ramp, not an instantaneous step. The `transient_event_score` of 0.80 is elevated but `candidate_event_blocks` is empty, so there are no discrete temporary events to clean. The primary changepoint at 2020-01-02 marks the end of the ramp, making `primary` the correct interval target. A ramp regressor added over this interval will allow the model to absorb the gradual level transition without misattributing it to trend extrapolation or seasonal misfit.
proposal.expected_effect: Adding a clipped ramp regressor over the primary candidate drift interval (2019-07-17 to 2020-01-02) will explicitly model the gradual level ascent, preventing the baseline Prophet trend from over-fitting the slope as a continuing upward trend. This should reduce forecast bias and lower MAE/RMSE below the naive full_history baseline (MAE ~23.5, WAPE ~12.2%) by correctly anchoring the post-ramp level without extrapolating the climb further into the forecast horizon.
